items.correct: The ability of different classes to respond to the same method call in their own way.
items.misconception: Having multiple classes with the same name.
items.random_wrong: Variables that change type at runtime.
items.why: Students confuse polymorphism with naming. Polymorphism means 'many forms' - a single interface (method signature) behaving differently depending on the object's actual class.

items.q: What is the main advantage of asymmetric encryption?
items.correct: Secure key exchange without sharing a secret in advance.
items.misconception: It is faster than symmetric encryption.
items.why: Students think asymmetric is just 'better'. It's actually SLOWER than symmetric. Its advantage is solving key distribution - the public key can be shared openly without compromising security.

items.q: What does inheritance allow a subclass to do?
items.correct: Reuse and extend the attributes and methods of its superclass.
items.random_wrong: Copy code from any unrelated class.
items.why: Students think inheritance overrides everything. It actually REUSES the parent's members (which can be added to or overridden), creating an 'is-a' relationship.
The answer examiners want is: Process. “Input” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Easier to solve. “Looks pretty” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many stages in 'Input - Process - Output'?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
